title: Pixel Weighting and Spatial Quantization Error
description:
Pixel Weighting and Spatial Quantization Error

Pixel Weighting and Spatial Quantization Error

Traditionally, images are segmented for blob analysis by applying a binarization threshold to the image: every pixel having a grey level above the threshold is part of the object and every pixel having a grey level below the threshold is part of the background.

There are some problems with this approach.  These problems arise from spatial quantization

errors that result in measurement inaccuracies.

In a pure binary system, the status (object/background) of every pixel on an object perimeter is uncertain.  The true object edge falls somewhere in the middle of the pixel.  Its position cannot be represented by the discrete pixel grid. Part of such a pixel is object and part is background.  The inability to represent accurately the status of such a pixel in a binarized image is one manifestation of spatial quantization error.

The examples below illustrate the effects of spatial quantization error.

In these examples, we assume that pixels covered less than 50% by the object have an area of 0 and that pixels covered 50% or more by the object have an area of 1.

Object position on a pixel grid affects the area of a blob

In both sets of figures, note how the area of the object changes as a result of a slight shift in the object position on the pixel grid. In the first set of figures, each object (square) is 8.5 x 8.5 pixels. In the top figure (on the left), the square is positioned on the grid so that each side covers 8 full pixels and extends an additional 0.25 pixels into the next pixel (which would not be counted toward the area), giving the square an area of 64 pixels. In the top figure (on the right), the square is positioned so that each side covers 7 full pixels and extends an additional 0.75 pixels into the next pixel (which would be counted toward the area), giving the square an area of 81 pixels.

Each vertical bar occupies 0.75 each of two adjacent pixels (left). Each vertical bar covers 1 full pixel plus 0.25 pixels on each side of that pixel (right).

The severity of spatial quantization errors depends on the ratio of perimeter to area in the image.  Note how much greater the effect of spatial quantization error becomes in the new figure, where there is a greater ratio of perimeter to area.  In this second set of figures, each vertical bar of the "H" is 1.5 pixels x 10 pixels. In the new figure (on the left), the H is positioned so that each vertical bar occupies 0.75 each of two adjacent pixels, meaning that each of the two pixels counts towards the area.  The horizontal bar covers 0.50 of 6 pixels, all contributing to the area. In the new figure (on the right), however, the H is positioned so that each vertical bar covers 1 full pixel plus 0.25 pixels on each side of that pixel; these 0.25 pixels do not count toward the area.  In the top figure (on the right), the horizontal bar covers 0.50 of 8 pixels (including the four partially covered by the vertical bars), all contributing to the area.

Edges that are aligned with the pixel grid (e.g., rectangles) tend to produce systematic reinforcing errors, while other edges (e.g., round objects) tend to product random, canceling errors.

The Blob object solves many of the problems associated with spatial quantization by providing a segmentation

mechanism,
				pixel weighting
			, that is more general than thresholding.  Pixel weighting allows segmentation of some scenes (like the one illustrated in the figure below) that cannot be segmented by thresholding, and reduces spatial quantization error in all scenes.  With this mechanism, each grey level can be assigned a status (object, background, or edge) independently depending on its weight.  Pixel weighting operates as follows: the weight W of a pixel is a fraction between 0 and 1.0; the pixel represents W units of object and (1 - W) units of background.

The weight of each pixel is determined by a weighting function (or map) that transforms every grey level into a weight.  A function such as this, in which each pixel value is treated uniformly throughout the image as being object, background or edge, may be constructed only for images that conform to the restrictions described above regarding segmentation.

Image which requires a weighting function to find the object
